Tomato_Bacterial_spot.features: Small dark brown spots on leaves and fruits surrounded by yellow halos.
Tomato_Bacterial_spot.cause: Caused by Xanthomonas bacteria which spread through infected seeds, water splash, and contaminated tools.
Tomato_Bacterial_spot.prevention: Use certified disease-free seeds, avoid overhead irrigation, rotate crops, and maintain good field sanitation.
Tomato_Bacterial_spot.cure: Apply copper-based bactericides and remove infected plant parts to prevent further spread.

Tomato_Early_blight.features: Dark brown spots with concentric rings forming a target pattern on older leaves.
Tomato_Early_blight.cause: Caused by the fungus Alternaria solani which thrives in warm and humid conditions.
Tomato_Early_blight.prevention: Practice crop rotation, keep foliage dry, provide proper spacing for airflow, and remove infected debris.
Tomato_Early_blight.cure: Apply fungicides such as chlorothalonil or copper fungicides regularly.

Tomato_healthy.features: Leaves appear green, firm, and free from spots or discoloration.
Tomato_healthy.cause: No disease present. The plant is healthy and growing normally.
Tomato_healthy.prevention: Maintain good agricultural practices such as proper watering, fertilization, and pest control.
Tomato_healthy.cure: No treatment required. Continue maintaining proper plant care.

Tomato_Late_blight.features: Large dark brown lesions on leaves with pale green edges and white fungal growth underneath.
Tomato_Late_blight.cause: Caused by the pathogen Phytophthora infestans which spreads rapidly in cool and moist weather.
Tomato_Late_blight.prevention: Avoid wet leaves, provide good airflow, remove infected plants, and use resistant varieties.
Tomato_Late_blight.cure: Apply fungicides containing copper or mancozeb to control the spread.

Tomato_Leaf_Mold.features: Yellow patches on upper leaf surfaces with olive-green mold on the underside.
Tomato_Leaf_Mold.cause: Caused by the fungus Passalora fulva which develops in humid greenhouse conditions.
Tomato_Leaf_Mold.prevention: Reduce humidity, improve ventilation, and avoid overhead watering.
Tomato_Leaf_Mold.cure: Apply fungicides and remove infected leaves to stop the spread.

Tomato_Septoria_leaf_spot.features: Small circular spots with gray centers and dark edges on leaves.
Tomato_Septoria_leaf_spot.cause: Caused by the fungus Septoria lycopersici which spreads through water splash and plant debris.
Tomato_Septoria_leaf_spot.prevention: Use crop rotation, remove infected leaves, and keep foliage dry.
Tomato_Septoria_leaf_spot.cure: Apply fungicides such as chlorothalonil or copper-based sprays.

Tomato_Spider_mites.features: Tiny yellow or white speckles on leaves and fine webbing on the underside.
Tomato_Spider_mites.cause: Caused by spider mites feeding on plant sap, usually in hot and dry conditions.
Tomato_Spider_mites.prevention: Maintain adequate humidity and regularly inspect plants for early signs of mites.
Tomato_Spider_mites.cure: Use insecticidal soaps, neem oil, or miticides to control the infestation.

Tomato_Target_Spot.features: Brown circular lesions with concentric rings that resemble a target pattern.
Tomato_Target_Spot.cause: Caused by the fungus Corynespora cassiicola which spreads in warm and humid environments.
Tomato_Target_Spot.prevention: Ensure proper plant spacing, remove infected debris, and practice crop rotation.
Tomato_Target_Spot.cure: Apply fungicides such as chlorothalonil or azoxystrobin.

Tomato_Tomato_mosaic_virus.features: Mosaic-like yellow and green mottling on leaves with distorted growth.
Tomato_Tomato_mosaic_virus.cause: Caused by Tomato Mosaic Virus which spreads through contaminated tools, seeds, and human handling.
Tomato_Tomato_mosaic_virus.prevention: Use virus-resistant varieties, disinfect tools, and avoid handling plants after tobacco use.
Tomato_Tomato_mosaic_virus.cure: There is no cure; remove and destroy infected plants immediately.

Tomato_Yellow_Leaf_Curl_Virus.features: Leaves curl upward and turn yellow with stunted plant growth.
Tomato_Yellow_Leaf_Curl_Virus.cause: Caused by Tomato Yellow Leaf Curl Virus transmitted by whiteflies.
Tomato_Yellow_Leaf_Curl_Virus.prevention: Control whitefly populations, use resistant varieties, and install insect screens.
Tomato_Yellow_Leaf_Curl_Virus.cure: Remove infected plants and control whiteflies using insecticides or neem oil.
